entry.title: Re: Cloud Transport Management for SAP BTP ABAP Environment – The Five-System Landscape
entry.content:
Hi Huijie,


With ImportUpTo, cTMS currently does not take into account branches from previous transports of the same software-component (SWC). When a transport for Software Component A (SWC A) is imported, referencing a specific branch and commit, the target tenant checks out exactly that commit. Any previous transports of the same SWC A are skipped. This is expected behavior, as a software component cannot be checked out to multiple commits from different branches at the same time.

One remark: 
We plan to introduce an enhancement that allows ImportUpTo to be executed for a selected software component only.
With this improvement, it will be possible to perform an ImportUpTo for transports related to SWC A, while previous transports of a different software component (for example, SWC B) can be imported independently at a later point in time. This enables a more flexible and selective import strategy, especially in landscapes where multiple software components are transported in parallel.

Best regards,

Sven

entry.title: Re: Characteristic Description Blank
entry.content:
Hi Vijay. Late last week I found a note https://me.sap.com/notes/3599670/E which we implemented yesterday in EQ1 and it resolved the problem. It was all about split records.

My basis colleague implemented the note which was really quick and immediately and without having to do any other activity the description was displaying

I got distracted last week because I was advised that it could have been something to do with UDT (User Defined Text) but that was not the case

Thanks very much for Vijay for your feedback

Best regards

Roberto

 

This is what the Note says ..


Symptom
Component: Product Safety
Module: Specification Management
You use transaction CGo2 or CGo2BD to call up Specification Management. You navigate to a specification and to a property.
You notice that the system does not display the descriptor for one or more classification system characteristics.

Reason and Prerequisites

Reason
Program error The classification system characteristic, for which the name is not displayed, has record splits.

Prerequisites
For information about the validity of the corrections, see the correction instructions.

Solution
The section "Support Packages" specifies the Support Packages that contain the corrections.
Alternatively, you can implement the attached correction instructions.
Note: Record splits are not supported by EHS for classification system characteristics. The functions of the classification
system supported by EHS are described in SAP Note 728636.

entry.title: SAP BusinessObjects 1000 Oracle IN limit in Prompts - How to do splitting in Universe?
entry.content:
Dear everyone,

We have a problem with SAP BusinessObjects 4.3 SP5. The users need to be able to search with up to 10k values in a single prompt field, but due to Oracle limitation, when trying to run a query with more than 1k values in a field, it yield an Oracle 1000 IN limit error. The UI input field itself seems to take a lot of values, we tried with 100k and after 30 sec of loading it showed them in the UI.

How to overcome this limit? Ideal would be, if there was a way to split the prompt before SAP BO send it to the database, maybe in the Universe/IDP tool, like a custom variable or function or script or whatever.

Something like this:

@prompt[0:999] OR

@prompt[1000:1999] OR

@prompt[2000:1999] OR

We know that we could add 10 input fields and the users could make the chunking themselves, but it's rather tedious if you need to do that multiple times a day. Also for our company it's not an option to let the users add these 10k values to the database first.

Any idea or other workaround appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Test Guidelines when extending solution using CLT
entry.content:
Good day @Vidhi_RamanChandra 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, there is guidance on how to test extensions that is built with Custom Logic. However, I would separate this into two points:

  1. Custom Logic Tracing is not the extension itself. It is mainly a troubleshooting and validation tool used to trace whether a BAdI implementation or custom logic is called, in which sequence it is called, and what parameter values are passed.
  2. Testing should still be done as business process testing, not only technical tracing. For end to end validation, the Test Automation Tool and the Test Your Process app to create, run, and evaluate automated business process tests.

So, I recommend you:

  • Use Custom Logic to implement the BAdI or business object logic.
  • Use Custom Logic Tracing to technically confirm that the logic is triggered and the right parameters are passed.
  • Use manual business process testing or Test Your Process to confirm that the final business outcome is correct.
  • Make sure you retest the extension after upgrades, transports, or changes to the BAdI interface.

What this means in simple terms, is:

  • Custom Logic Tracing tells you whether the custom logic was called and with which values.
  • It does not replace functional, regression, integration, or UAT testing.
  • The real test should be based on the full business process where the extension is used.
  • If the extension affects posting, pricing, output, workflow, approval, or master data, the complete process should be tested from input to final document.

Here are references for more guiding details.

2699678 - How To Troubleshoot Custom Business Objects Logic - SAP for Me

3354518 - Custom Logic Tracing app: "Either this method does not have any parameters or an error occurred in the method call" - SAP for Me

3618748 - BAdI APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS does not have any importing parameters - SAP for Me

3648982 - Error message "Implementation must be republished" after upgrade in SAP S/4HANA Cloud BAdi - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Source Layer of “Your session has expired” Message in SSAM Mobile Application
entry.content:
Description

We are experiencing an issue in the SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM) mobile application where users receive the following message when attempting to access the application:

“Your session has expired”

The message appears during application access/usage and forces users to re-authenticate.

Clarification Required

We would like expert assistance in identifying the exact originating layer of this message within the SSAM technical architecture.

Specifically, we need confirmation whether this message is triggered from:

  • SAP MDK mobile client layer
  • SAP BTP Mobile Services layer
  • Authentication provider (IAS / SAML / OAuth / Azure AD)
  • SAP Gateway / OData service layer
  • Backend S/4HANA session management

Purpose of Request

Understanding the originating layer will help us:

  • Perform targeted troubleshooting
  • Validate session timeout configurations across components
  • Avoid unnecessary changes in unaffected layers

System Details

  • Application: SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM)
  • Mobile Framework: SAP MDK
  • Backend: SAP S/4HANA
  • Middleware: SAP BTP Mobile Services
  • Authentication: (Please specify – OAuth / SAML / Basic / Certificate)

entry.title: Re: Why data fragmentation limits AI in SAP landscapes
entry.content:
Good point, Nicolas. And it is not limited to SAP landscapes, it is a general challenge with business data. If your data is a mess, AI will scale that mess. You describe the consequences, that is business is losing trust.

The "garbage in, garbage out" aspect only touches the surface. It is the data architecture and semantics that must be solved. "Single source of truth" was the tag line a while ago. A number of SAP customers understood this strategic opportunity and successfully managed transformation during their move to S/4. Blogs and online articles indicate that attention is getting back on that tag line again.

entry.title: Succession - Removing a readiness rating scale option
entry.content:
HI everyone, I'm wondering if anyone has had any experience in the past of removing a rating scale option from their Readiness rating scale? Currently, we have a 4-point readiness rating scale:

Emergency Replacement Only
Ready Now
Ready Soon
Ready Longer Term

With the new Emergency Cover feature which was introduced a few years ago, we're considering enabling this and removing the 'Emergency Replacement Only' readiness value, so that people can mark successors as (for example) Ready Longer Term but also flag them as Emergency Cover. 

I know there are various places where this would need to be updated, including on the provisioning/XML side which we'd need partner support for anyway, but I'm keen to hear if anyone has done this in the past and is able to share their experiences/challenges/pitfalls so I can go into this with as much information as possible!

Many thanks in advance, 
Jack

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026: Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions
entry.content:
Hello everyone, I am Noor Zehra Naqvi (many people call me Noor),  a Senior Director of Product Management at SAP Signavio. My focus area is where AI, data, and analytics meet.

Today I would like to introduce my SAP Sapphire session:

Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions | BTM1573

In the session I’ll share our strategy about AI Agent Excellence: how to discover, govern, maintain, and continuously improve agents so they deliver real value in business processes.

AI agents are becoming a new reality. Companies are increasing their investments, and we’re seeing more and more agents show up as part of the workforce, not only as a concept, but in real operational workflows.

From there, the key question becomes: where should you apply agents, and how do you scale them responsibly? This is where SAP Signavio helps by giving agents the right process and business context, and by supporting the guardrails organizations need so agents remain compliant, predictable, and aligned with how the business actually runs.

We’ll also dive into governance and transparency, how you can understand the impact of agents on your end-to-end processes, and how to validate conformance. For example, through what we call agent mining, you can see where agents touch a process, what variants they introduce, and whether those variants are conforming or not. This level of visibility is essential when you move from a few pilots to broad adoption.

To keep it practical, one of our customers, Albatha Group will join us to share how SAP Signavio supported their AI and AI Agents journey and helped create value in their organization. We’ll close with a sneak peek at our latest releases on Agentic AI.

Interested in learning more?

 

My biggest recommendation is: get started and experiment. Try out AI agents, see what’s possible, and quite literally get your hands dirty. At the same time, scaling agents inside an organization requires a framework: strong security, clear control mechanisms, and governance that prevents unexpected or unwanted behavior.

That’s why it’s worth investing time in understanding how SAP Signavio supports this journey, so you’re set up not just to build agents, but to operate them with confidence.

Also, beyond my own session), I’d encourage you to explore a few other Signavio-related sessions at SAP Sapphire, especially those that help you connect strategy with execution:

  • Unlocking the new frontier of agentic transformation with SAP Signavio | BTM2176
  • Deliver measurable transformation value confidently with SAP Signavio | BTM1574
  • Build a transformation capability that delivers measurable value | BTM1286
  • Crafting the future: The SAP Signavio solutions 2026 road map | BTM1715

 

Now let’s hear from you!

 

In the comments’ section, it would be great to hear where you are on your agentic AI journey:

  • How far along are you, are you still piloting, or already scaling?
  • Roughly how many agents do you have running today?
  • What are the biggest struggles, blockers and risks you’re seeing as you scale?

 

If you’re attending SAP Sapphire, I hope you’ll join the conversation, both during the event and in the comments. The shift to agentic AI is exciting, but the organizations that win will be the ones that pair innovation with strong process context, governance, and transparency.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA configuration for SAC pull of plan data
entry.content:
Hello Sivaramakrishnan,

Thank you for your feedback.

Based on your explanation my understanding is the following (and please correct if I am wrong):
(A) The revenue planning of the different lines of business are split into different SAC models.
(B) Contrary all lines of business do their cost planning in one central OPEX model.
(C) Revenue planning (from the lines of business/SAC model) and cost planning (from the one central model) are finally consolidated into another central model.

Within revenue planning (A), I foresee no problems as long as the different Lines of Business (which plan on the same revenue accounts) are distinguished via a specific S/4 field (representing the line of business), and of course, this S/4 field must be in addition included into the plan data scope.

Similarly, revenue planning (from the 10 different SAC models) and OPEX planning can be written into the same planning category as different GL accounts are used for revenue and cost planning (and GL account being part of the plan data scope).  However, ensure that any previously planned GL accounts (which were subsequently deleted in SAC) are correctly reflected in S/4 (i.e. with GL account being part of the Plan Data Scope).

In contrast, data within the consolidated financial model may overlap if plan data is (most probably) written to the same GL accounts used for revenue and OPEX planning. Moreover, it would not make sense to map the data from the Consolidated Financial Model (C) to the same category used for Revenue (A) and Cost Planning (B), as S/4 reporting could potentially display duplicate values. Therefore, it must always be clarified whether, from a reporting perspective, it is necessary/correct to write the data into a single planning category.

If the assumptions I made above are incorrect, then I want to point out that cross-combinations of the (plan data scope) field values are taken as well into account to replace the existing S/4 data (as described in section 2 under Technical Settings) with the consequence that a configuration ID (related to one model) can overwrite the data of another configuration ID (associated to another model).

Thank you.

Kind regards,
Tarkan Citirgi

P.S. The provided "import" templates (as part of our app Import Financial Plan Data) work primarily with a single planning category (the SAP-delivered planning category PLN); the budget-specific categories used trigger additional budget-specific functionalities which explains why they are utilized in the budget specific import templates.

entry.title: Re: What is the difference between a review booklet and a...
entry.content:
Good day @db11 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: What are the differences between reports and booklets, and how does SAP intend them to be used?

Answer: A report is usually best for focused analysis of one query; KPI, or dataset. A Review Booklet on the other hand is better for a guided business review across multiple pages, for example cost center review, financial statement review, group reporting review, budget review, profitability review, or period-end analysis.


Question 2: How many of the current query reports/which areas of applications are planned to be replaced with review booklets? How long will the transition take?

Answer: I am not aware of any SAP statement that confirms that all query reports will be replaced by Review Booklets, and there is no fixed transition timeline for a full replacement. SAP generally approaches this incrementally. In other words, Review Booklets are on release-by-release base, while query reports and analytical queries remain available and relevant.

I recommend you:

  • List the query reports your business currently uses.
  • Check whether a Review Booklet already exists for the same business area in Manage KPIs and Reports.  
     
  • Use dimension, filters, hierarchies, drilldowns to compare the Review Booklet with the current report. Exporting requirements is also an option.
  • You can use the Review Booklet where your business needs a guided review flow.
  • Keep your existing query report where the Review Booklet does not yet cover the required reporting your business needs. 
  • You can use Review Booklet Designer in Manage KPIs and Report to copy, or create a Review Booklet.

Here are references for more guidance

3730170 - FAQ for Cost Center Review Booklet in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3512310 - Not assigned cost center hierarchy in APP 'Cost Center review booklet' - SAP for Me

3595605 - Lease and Real Estate Accounting Review Booklet App not displaying data - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: International Design Thinking Training & CoachCertification Bootcamp
entry.content:
For the first time, Impact Week Armenia 2026 is bringing a global Design Thinking training and certification program to Yerevan — creating a rare opportunity for Armenia’s business, education, and innovation community.

Programs of this level, usually delivered by such experienced innovation consultants and experts from SAP,  UnternehmerTUM, and former HPI program leadership, are typically priced much higher internationally. This year, because the bootcamp is taking place in Armenia, the program has been made highly affordable for local participants.

This is a non-profit initiative: all participation fees will directly support scholarships for 80+ young Armenian AI talents, helping build the next generation of innovators, entrepreneurs, and leaders of Armenia's AI ecosystem.

More details are available here:
https://gamma.app/docs/IMPACT-Week-Armenia-2026-6zz1zqzzvvwdqtm?mode=doc

Participants can choose between two tracks:

  • Design Thinking Coach Certification
    June 22–27 + 12 hours of online preparation | Application deadline: May 10
    A certification track for professionals who want to facilitate Design Thinking workshops, guide teams, and bring structured innovation methods into their organizations.
  •  4-Day Design Thinking Bootcamp
    June 24–27 | Application deadline: June 10
    A hands-on innovation training where participants work in teams on real-life challenges and learn how to move from problem understanding to solution concepts and prototypes.

This can be especially valuable for companies that want to strengthen internal innovation capacity, train future facilitators, support product and service development, or expose their teams to internationally tested innovation methods.

We are open to partnership and sponsorship opportunities with companies that would like to support this initiative and contribute to strengthening Armenia’s entrepreneurship and innovation capacity.

In case of any questions, please feel free to contact Carolin Taenzer -https://www.linkedin.com/in/carolin-taenzer/ or  Tsovinar Chugaszyan -  https://www.linkedin.com/in/tsovinarc/?skipRedirect=true

entry.content:
Hi @Joana_Rochete 

It's hard to get these answers, even for us in services at SAP. Even if you create a ticket to support on this it will be hard for them to get that answer unless you are able to point to some gap between your testing and the implementation guide.

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to include Freight Charges in MAP without creating an Account Payable?
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

I am currently working on an S/4HANA Public Cloud implementation and I have a specific requirement regarding Freight Charges in Transportation Management (TM) that I haven't been able to solve using standard configuration.

The Scenario: In our Transport Order, we need to include two specific Charge Types:

  1. SF04 - Basic Rate: Standard freight cost.

  2. ZB01 - Fuel to Carrier (Custom): A charge representing fuel provided physically by the company to the carrier.

The Requirement: Both charges must increase the Moving Average Price (MAP) of the materials being transported. The system is currently performing the Accrual Posting correctly, increasing the inventory value.

The Problem: The charge ZB01 (Fuel) should NOT create an open item in Accounts Payable (MIRO).

  • The fuel is provided in-kind from our own stock (consumed via MIGO against a Cost Center), so we do not owe this amount to the carrier.

  • If we include this line in the MIRO, we create an incorrect liability.

  • If we remove the line or set it to zero during MIRO, the system triggers a cost adjustment that reduces the material's MAP, reversing the initial (and correct) accrual.

What we have explored:

  • We looked into making the Charge Type statistical, but in the current Public Cloud version, this setting seems to be global/header-level and not specific per Charge Type (unlike Condition Types in MM).

  • We need the inventory to absorb the cost, but the offsetting entry should ideally go against a G/L account (like a fuel consumption/offset account) instead of the Supplier BP.

Technical Question: Has anyone faced a similar requirement where a Landed Cost needs to affect the material valuation but should be excluded from the Supplier Invoice (MIRO) without reversing the cost in the material master?

Any workarounds, specific Account Key configurations, or insights on how to handle "in-kind" transport charges in Public Cloud would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance for your help!

Best regards,

Sergio Bravo

entry.title: Re: CDS Doubles - Writing Unit Tests for ABAP CDS
entry.content:
If ABAP code accesses both tables directly and CDS views which use some of these tables, use the CDS test environment, mock the tables only and call the method enable_double_redirection:

environment->enable_double_redirection( ).

Source: ABAP SQL TDF and CDS TDF | SAP Help Portal 

NB: enabling the double redirection of the ABAP SQL test environment (CL_OSQL_TEST_ENVIRONMENT) didn't work for me (ABAP 7.58 SP 1), the CDS view doesn't use the mocked tables, it works only if you mock the CDS view itself, it reads the CDS mocked data, not the mocked data of its tables.

entry.title: Re: Date Override field not recognized in Compensation fo...
entry.content:
Thank you for your help @xavierlegarrec!

With a combination of your formulas, I was able to implement the requirement successfully, and the customFinalAdvancementDateOverride field is now working as expected.

The main difficulty I encountered was that, unlike other field types, the system does not seem able to properly recognize when a Date field is empty.

Because of that, a simple logic such as: If customFinalAdvancementDateOverride is empty -> show Current Advancement Date.  Otherwise -> show customFinalAdvancementDateOverride was not working correctly.

To work around this limitation, I created a custom field called DATE_DIFFERENCE with the following formula: dateDiff(CurrentAdvancementDate, customFinalAdvancementDateOverride).

My logic was: If the result returned either a positive or negative value, it meant that an Override Date existed. If the formula returned N/A, it meant the Override field was empty (as the comparison could not be evaluated).

Then, in the Final Advancement Date field, I used the following logic: If DATE_DIFFERENCE > 0 or < 0 -> show customFinalAdvancementDateOverride. Otherwise -> show CurrentAdvancementDate. With this approach, the field started working correctly.

However, I found an additional edge case: If the employee does not have a CurrentAdvancementDate, but the planner still wants to insert an Override Date, the DATE_DIFFERENCE field returns N/A, because the comparison cannot be performed. To solve this, I created another CurrentAdvancementDate (mapped to EC). However, instead of configuring it as a Date type field, I configured it as a String type field. This allowed me to identify whether the EC field contained a value or was empty, and complete the logic successfully.

entry.title: LOOP AT internal_table STEP — Because Life Is Too Short to Write Counter Variables
entry.content:
Simple but annoying.

Imagine the situation like, it is Monday morning. You have a fresh cup of coffee, positive attitude and simple ABAP task: loop through an internal table backwards. In general, sounds easy and when you open code editor then reality hits. Personally, I would be lazy to add some extra counters and start thinking how can I make it more beautiful and easy. Previously you would end-up with something similar as:

DATA(lv_index) = lines( lt_orders ).

WHILE lv_index > 0.
  READ TABLE lt_orders INDEX lv_index INTO DATA(ls_order).
  " ... adding some logic here ...
  lv_index -= 1.
ENDWHILE.

It works. Nobody is arguing that, but looking at the code, something from my heart will question can we make it better and don't even get me started on what happens with hashed table. That's a story for a different blog post and a stronger beverage 😉

Good News: Someone at SAP Was Listening

Starting with ABAP 7.57, a new addition called STEP arrived for LOOP AT. And honestly? It's one of those features that makes you think "Now, it is show time" as Johnny Cage.

The idea is beautifully simple. You tell LOOP AT how many rows to jump at a time, and in which direction. That's it.

LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) STEP n.
  " Your logic goes here ...
ENDLOOP.

The value of n  does two things at once:

  • The sign (positive or negative) controls the direction of the loop
  • The absolute value controls the step size (how many rows to skip)

Going Forward, But Make It Sparse

Let's say you have 10 rows and you only want to process every second one. No problem:

LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) STEP 2.
  " Processes rows 1, 3, 5, 7, 9
ENDLOOP.

Going Backwards — The Main Event

Now for the exciting part. To loop through a table in reverse order, you just use a negative value:

LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) STEP -1.
  " Processes rows: last → first
ENDLOOP.

Mixing STEP with FROM and TO

You can also combine STEP  with FROM and TO to narrow down the range. But here's the part that catches people off guard: when using a negative STEP, FROM must be greater than TO.

" Process rows 8 down to 3, going backwards
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) FROM 8 TO 3 STEP -1.
  " Processes rows: 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3
ENDLOOP.

What about sy-tabix? Good question, it behaves exactly as you'd expect, namely it reflects to the actual row number in the table, not just some counter that goes 1, 2, 3 and so on. Besides it works with DELETE statement as well.

That's it. No counter. No WHILE. No READ TABLE. No cold coffee 😊

Quick Reference

What you want to doHow to write it

Process every 2nd row

LOOP AT itab ... STEP 2

Process in reverse order

LOOP AT itab ... STEP -1

Reverse, skip every other

LOOP AT itab ... STEP -2

Reverse within a range

LOOP AT itab ... FROM 8 TO 3 STEP -1

Delete every 3rd row

DELETE itab STEP 3

Happy coding.

P. S. I didn't find blog describing the LOOP AT ... STEP, so If you find something similar, please feel free to share in the comment section. I will be happy to check it as well.

entry.title: Max Success Plans Help Keep HR Transformation on Track
entry.content:
Every HR transformation starts with intent: there’s a clear ambition on the table to modernise HR, simplify processes, improve employee experience, get better insights, prepare for what comes next.  

The roadmap looks solid. The program is funded. The partners are aligned. Momentum is high.  And then reality kicks in: priorities shift, a regulatory requirement appears, a business unit needs to move faster than planned, a country goes live later (or earlier) than expected, a “temporary” workaround is introduced to keep things moving. Six months later, another decision builds on top of it. Another release arrives. Someone asks - is this the right time now to think about analytics? Or AI? Or extensions?  

None of these moments feel like failure. In fact, most of them feel entirely reasonable at the time. But taken together, they slowly pull the transformation off its original course. That’s what makes large‑scale HR transformation so difficult.  

It rarely breaks in one obvious place. It drifts: quietly, incrementally, and often invisibly, until the organisation finds itself with a solution that technically works, but is harder to scale, harder to govern, and harder to evolve than intended.  

Max Success Plan exists for exactly this stage of the journey. 

The Hidden Risk in Long‑Running HR Transformations 

Once the initial excitement of an HR transformation gives way to delivery, the nature of risk quietly changes. 

HR transformation is rarely a single project with a clear start and end. In most organisations, it unfolds as a multi‑phase journey spanning discovery, design, implementation, deployment, and long‑term operations - often over several years. During that time, teams are under constant pressure to adapt scope, accelerate delivery, make “temporary” compromises to keep momentum, introduce local variations, or respond to new regulatory and business demands.  

Taken individually, these decisions usually make sense. They help teams move forward, meet urgent needs, or unblock progress. The risk doesn’t come from any one choice, it comes from what happens when no one is actively safeguarding how those choices fit together over time. 

The impact is rarely dramatic or immediate. There is no single moment where the transformation is declared a failure. Instead, subtle issues begin to accumulate: architectural inconsistencies emerge, data models fragment, custom code and extensions multiply, user experience decisions stop scaling, and operational friction increases after go‑live. Each issue on its own feels manageable. Together, they quietly undermine the original transformation goals. 

By the time these problems become visible, addressing them is often expensive, disruptive, and politically difficult. The organisation is already live, already relying on the solution, and already tied into processes that are hard to unwind. 

This is the point where many HR leaders realise that momentum alone isn’t enough. What’s missing isn’t effort or expertise - it’s continuity. Someone needs to hold the transformation together across phases, decisions, and stakeholders, ensuring that short‑term progress doesn’t come at the cost of long‑term success. 

Transformation Needs Continuity, Not Just Momentum 

This is the point where many HR transformations begin to diverge. 

Most organisations are very good at generating momentum. They mobilise teams, align partners, fund programs, and push hard toward go‑live milestones. Considerable effort is invested in getting designs approved, configurations built, data migrated, and employees trained. But once go‑live is achieved, attention often shifts elsewhere: toward stabilisation, operations, and the next set of immediate business priorities. 

What tends to receive far less focus is what happens after the milestone is reached. 

In reality, the hardest part of HR transformation is not getting through individual phases, but maintaining continuity of intent as the organisation moves from design to build, from build to deploy, from deploy to run, and from run into continuous change. Each transition introduces new stakeholders, new constraints, and new pressures. Without a deliberate mechanism to carry decisions forward, the original transformation logic begins to fragment. 

This is where Max Success Plan becomes fundamentally different from other engagement models. 

It is not designed to accelerate individual tasks or optimise isolated activities. Its purpose is to protect the transformation itself, by providing sustained oversight, expert safeguarding, and architectural continuity across phases. Instead of treating each stage as a handover point, Max treats the transformation as a single, evolving journey, where decisions made early are continuously validated against long‑term goals. 

This shift - from momentum‑driven delivery to continuity‑driven transformation - is what allows complex HR programs to remain coherent, scalable, and governable over time. 

What Max Success Plan Actually Safeguards 

 

Max Success Plan builds on both the Foundational and Advanced Success Plans, but it adds something that long‑running HR transformations consistently lack: expert oversight of strategic decisions across the full lifecycle. 

This safeguarding spans the areas where drift and risk most often accumulate over time. It includes: 

  • HR architecture and hybrid cloud strategy, where early technical choices determine long‑term scalability 
  • It covers data migration and data volume management, ensuring quality and performance don’t erode as complexity grows 
  • It extends into UX and analytics strategy, so employee experience and insight capabilities remain coherent rather than fragmented 
  • It addresses integration and operational readiness, where small inconsistencies can create persistent friction 
  • And increasingly, it applies to AI, innovation, and extensibility decisions, where experimentation without guardrails can quickly outpace readiness.  
  • Just as importantly, it provides governance across partners and internal teams, helping align multiple contributors around a shared transformation intent. 

Max customers gain access to HCM Solution Architects, Coaches, and Experts‑on‑Demand who engage across the entire journey, from Discover through Run, not only at isolated checkpoints. Their role is not to take over delivery or replace implementation partners. Instead, they act as an independent layer of continuity, ensuring that the right decisions are made at the right time, with full visibility into downstream impact across systems, processes, and future phases. 

This is what allows complex HR transformations to evolve without losing coherence, and why Max is less about accelerating execution, and more about protecting the transformation as it unfolds. 

Keeping Transformations on Track Means Protecting Decisions 

Continuity becomes even harder to preserve as transformations progress, not because teams stop caring, but because the volume and frequency of decisions increase. Over time, the transformation demands constant judgement calls, often under pressure, often with incomplete information. This is where many HR programs begin to struggle, not due to lack of capability, but due to decision fatigue. 

One of the most common (and least visible) failure points in HR transformations is decision fatigue. 

As complexity grows, teams naturally start optimising locally. To keep delivery moving, a quick integration workaround is introduced. A UX shortcut is taken to meet a deadline. A data migration compromise is accepted to reduce immediate risk. An extension is built “just for now” because it seems easier than redesigning a process. Each of these decisions is rational in isolation, and often necessary at the time. 

The problem isn’t the decision itself, it’s the accumulation. Without transformation‑level safeguarding, these local optimisations stack up over months and years, quietly introducing technical debt and operational risk. What began as a coherent design starts to fragment. Integrations become harder to maintain. User experience loses consistency. Data quality and governance weaken. And fixing these issues later requires far more effort than preventing them in the first place. 

This is why Max Success Plan introduces structured checkpoints and expert reviews: not as bureaucracy, but as protection. Phase reviews, architecture points of view, feasibility checks, and value design governance exist to take the pressure off individual teams and ensure that short‑term progress does not undermine long‑term success. They create deliberate moments where decisions are reviewed in context, re‑validated against transformation goals, and adjusted before drift becomes embedded. 

In long‑running HR programs, this kind of decision support isn’t a slowdown. It’s what keeps the transformation intact as complexity increases. 

Why This Matters More as AI Enters HR 

AI accelerates everything, including risk. 

When organisations begin exploring AI‑enabled HR use cases, the margin for error narrows significantly. Decisions that once felt reversible suddenly are not. Data quality becomes foundational rather than “good enough.” Architectural choices shape what is possible for years to come. Governance expectations increase as AI touches sensitive people processes. Questions of trust, transparency, and ethics move from abstract discussions into day‑to‑day reality. 

In this context, the consequences of drift multiply. An architectural shortcut taken earlier can limit which AI capabilities can be adopted later. Fragmented data models undermine confidence in AI‑driven insights. Extensions built without a long‑term strategy become blockers rather than accelerators. And once AI is embedded into HR processes, undoing a poor decision is far more complex than avoiding it in the first place. 

This is where Max Success Plan plays a critical role. It helps organisations explore AI and innovation in a controlled, value‑driven way, ensuring that experimentation does not outpace readiness or compromise the foundation of the HR landscape. By combining expert oversight, architectural safeguarding, and structured governance, Max enables organisations to move forward with AI confidently, without sacrificing the integrity of the transformation they’ve already invested in. 

Transformation That Survives Contact with Reality 

Max Success Plan customers aren’t trying to simplify their environments by pretending complexity doesn’t exist. They are operating directly inside it: managing global HR processes, hybrid landscapes, long‑running programs, and continuous change as a normal state of business. The difference is intent. 

Instead of reacting to drift after it becomes visible, Max focuses on keeping the transformation on track while the business keeps moving. Decisions are reviewed in context, trade‑offs are made consciously, and the downstream impact of each choice is understood before it becomes embedded. 

Over time, this changes the character of the transformation. There are fewer painful course corrections, clearer governance across teams and partners, and HR platforms that remain scalable and coherent instead of slowly fragmenting. Most importantly, the solution continues to support strategic ambition long after the initial go‑live celebration has passed, when the real work of transformation actually begins. 

Is Max the Right Conversation to Have? 

At some point in a long‑running HR transformation, the questions change. 

It’s no longer just about whether the system works or whether teams are keeping up. The real question becomes whether the transformation can continue to scale, adapt, and evolve without losing coherence. That’s usually when organisations start recognising patterns in their own journey. 

If your HR transformation spans multiple years or phases, runs across SAP and non‑SAP systems, involves multiple partners and stakeholder groups, includes global payroll, data migration, or an emerging AI strategy, and must remain scalable and governable over time, then the challenges you’re facing are not tactical, they are structural. 

In that context, Max Success Plan isn’t about “more services” or additional delivery capacity. It’s about safeguarding the transformation you’re already investing in, by protecting architectural intent, decision quality, and operational readiness as complexity increases. 

For many customers, the right next step isn’t a technical deep dive or another milestone review. It’s a strategic conversation. A conversation with your Customer Success Manager or Account Executive to assess where your transformation is today, what risks are starting to emerge, and whether Max Success Plan is the right engagement model for where your HR landscape is heading next. 

Because when HR transformation becomes continuous, keeping it on track requires more than momentum. It requires intent, and the right structure around it.

entry.title: Re: Support Pack move to 608 vs. EHP8 upgrade?
entry.content:
Before implementing PCC in your system, verify the SP-Level in System > Status: Product Version. 

If SP-Level is lower, you should request to apply the missing SP-Level at least to your system administrator, but consider that a new PCC framework called "PCC Manage Activities" has been released and is available only for S4Hana system, so maybe it has sense to wait until moving to ECP.

New ECP systems are delivered in S4Hana with the latest version.

Best Regards

Giorgio

entry.title: S4HC Public: From Cost Collectors to WBS Elements
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We are implementing S4HC Public to a client who produces cement and concrete: 

  • They are currently on SAP R/3 and use cost collectors.

  • They perform periodic allocations and distributions to orders that have an associated cost collector.

  • In S/4HANA Cloud Public, the standard setup does not allow to use the order as a receiver for those allocations; the standard configuration only allow cost centers or WBS elements as receivers.

  • Their real process looks more like monthly cost accumulation and periodic settlement.

¿Would it be a good idea to propose to this client to switch from cost collectors to WBS elements? Can WBS elements be periodically settled to a materials?

entry.title: API for stock transfer for EWM based warehouse for Product & batch in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Team,
We are in public cloud landscape, where we are looking for a standard API which can do internal stock transfer for an EWM based warehouse for a product and batch combination. We are open to other option from Handling Unit also to do stock type transfer. The material posting api will not work here and we are looking something specific to EWM. The class and interface starting with /SCWM will also not work as we are on 2602CE version. Please help with the API which can do the stock type movement.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: API for stock transfer for EWM based warehouse for Pr...
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, there is currently no released standard public API specifically designed for EWM internal stock transfer or stock type movement at product/batch level comparable to the classic /SCWM function modules or classes available in private cloud/on-premise systems.

However, did you checked the APIs below?

I would recommend using the API Change Warehouse Physical Stock Type by Product. This API allows you to change the stock type of a product within the warehouse. Here is a brief overview of how you can use this API:

API Overview

  • HTTP Method: POST
  • Entity: WarehousePhysicalStockProducts

Request Properties

  • EWMStockType: Mandatory
  • EWMGoodsMovementEntryQuantity: Mandatory
  • EWMGoodsMovementEntryUnit: Mandatory

Example Request

--batch_01869434-0006 
Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary=changeset_id-1660905366757-4138 
--changeset_id-1660905366757-4138 
Content-Type: application/http 
Content-Transfer-Encoding: binary 
Content-ID: 0.0 
POST WarehousePhysicalStockProducts(ParentHandlingUnitUUID={{ParentHandlingUnitUUID}},StockItemUUID={{StockItemUUID}})/SAP__self.ChangeStockType HTTP/1.1 
Content-Type:application/json 
{ 
"EWMStockType": "B", 
"EWMGoodsMovementEntryQuantity": 1, 
"EWMGoodsMovementEntryUnit": "PC" 
} 
--changeset_id-1660905366757-4138-- 
--batch_01869434-0006--

Response

The operation returns the remaining stock after the stock type change. This API is suitable for scenarios where you need to change the stock type within an EWM-managed warehouse, and it supports handling unit-based operations as well.

 

In addition, I would recommend checking the API below: Change Product or Batch for Warehouse Physical Stock by Product | SAP Help Portal  and Change Stock Owner or Usage for Warehouse Physical Stock by Product | SAP Help Portal

For all the options available regarding API in Warehousing, please check:

APIs for Warehousing | SAP Help Portal

Specifically for your requirement, I would recommend checking for our standard options in the links below:

Operations for Warehouse Physical Stock by Product API | SAP Help Portal

Operations for Warehouse Order and Task API | SAP Help Portal

 

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Dynamic Properties for Custom Services
entry.content:
Managing complex business requirements often requires flexibility in how fields behave across different scenarios. Today, we're excited to announce that Dynamic Properties are now supported for custom entities created via custom service settings. Dynamic Properties allow administrators to define field behavior (like mandatory, visibility, readonly) rules that adapt based on business conditions.

 

For Standard Entities - Dynamic Properties are available in Extensibility Administration

For Custom Services – It is supported at root entity level and Dynamic Properties configuration is accessible during New entity creation or editing existing entities.

When you create or edit a root entity with custom services, you'll get a new Dynamic Property tab for root entity.

Example scenario:

Your organization uses a custom Work Order service. You need to ensure that whenever a work order is set to Active status, providing a Start Date becomes mandatory. This ensures critical scheduling information is never missed for active work orders.

To configure this as an admin user- Navigate to custom entity (custom services) and Access the Dynamic Property Tab

 Click on + (new) Field Attributes.

 

Define condition rule and property assignments:

Behaviour at runtime

entry.title: Re: Crystal Report duplicating rows
entry.content:
Check your table joins. Most likely, you are joining to a different table that might contain 2 matches, causing the record duplication.

If that was not the issue, then check section structure. You may have 2 details sections (Detail a and Detail b) with a section suppression condition that is supposed to cause one to show and one to suppress. But the logic is failing, causing both sections to show. If that is the issue, Crystal should show only 3 as the total row count (in the status bar).

entry.title: Re: Too New to Rate in Variable Pay not showing -1971
entry.content: @Joana_Rochete - I will add the fields, pmRating and totalIndividualRating are not string fields, they are numeric. This may add to the system confusing the results. If you wish to convert the results to string, you should use the toString function, i.e. toString(pmRating). Also, because of this, the conditional logic you have in your formula has a discrepancy in that the use of single quotes would indicate the field is a string field but in fact, it is numeric. You may need to adjust some formulas to account for this. As Xavier noted, it is difficult capture a specific answer.

entry.title: Reducing Bias with Calibration Alerts in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
In today's HR landscape, ensuring equitable talent decisions and retaining top performers is a C-suite priority. Calibration sessions are intended to ensure fair and consistent talent decisions across an organization. However, even with the best intentions, unconscious bias can influence outcomes.

In a recent client-side HR transformation rollout, we realized their calibration sessions had devolved into purely administrative exercises. Despite having structured Performance Management forms, managers often arrived unprepared, relying heavily on recency bias or 'gut feeling' rather than historical data. What prompted us to implement Calibration Alerts for this environment was an internal talent audit: we discovered a pattern where several sustained high performers hadn't seen a promotion or lateral move in over three years, yet this wasn't being flagged during talent reviews. We needed a system-driven way to force a cognitive pause and turn these client sessions into data-backed talent discussions rather than subjective debates.

SAP SuccessFactors Calibration Alerts provide a data-driven nudge to facilitators when predefined patterns or anomalies are detected during a live session. This article outlines the end-to-end configuration steps required to implement Calibration Alerts in a Performance and Goals environment.

 

Prerequisites

Before you begin configuration, ensure the following requirements are met.

Provisioning & Feature Enablement


 Employee Central, Calibration, MDF, and Intelligent Services must be enabled in your instance.

Additionally, navigate to Admin Center > Manage Calibration Settings > Global Settings and enable the toggle for Calibration Alert (Beta).

Metadata Framework (MDF) Permissions

  • Configure Object Definitions
  • Access To Business Rule Execution Log
  • Configure Business Rules
  • Manage Data for Alert Message and Sequence objects
  • Import permission on Metadata Framework

Note: While the rule can evaluate historical data from up to three prior forms, the active Calibration Session must be based on a single Performance Form Template ID for alerts to fire correctly.

Configuring the Alert Message

Alert messages define the text displayed to facilitators and others when a rule condition is met. Messages should be neutral, actionable.

Navigation: Admin Center > Manage Data > Create New > Alert Message

Example Configuration

External Code:  Alert_NoPromotion

Message Text: This subject has received a High Performer rating for two consecutive cycles but does not have a recorded promotion or lateral move in the last 36 months. Consider discussing career progression during this session.
Step 2.1: In the Manage Data tool, click the Create New dropdown and select Alert Message to begin configuring your custom alert text.

 

Step 2.2: Define your custom alert by assigning an external code, localizing the alert message text.

During the initial design phase, we quickly realized that tone matters just as much as the data. We intentionally drafted these messages to be objective and strictly actionable. The goal is to gently nudge facilitators with facts, rather than making managers feel like the system is second-guessing their authority.

Building the Calibration Alert Rule

Create a new business rule of type Calibration Alert. This rule type is specifically designed to evaluate subjects during an active calibration session.


Navigation: Admin Center > Configure Business Rules > Create New Rule > Calibration Alert


Step 3.1: Select the Calibration Alert scenario and define your rule's Name, ID, and Description

 Step 3.2: Map the performance form templates for the last three years in the designated dropdown fields, then click Continue to proceed to the logic configuration.

Take extra care during this mapping step. A frequent trap when configuring these rules is accidentally selecting cloned or outdated template IDs instead of the actual historical forms. Always double-check your IDs; otherwise, the alert will silently fail to evaluate the correct past performance data.

 Step 3.3: Define your If/Then logic

When building out these parameters, simpler is better. We originally experimented with highly complex nested conditions, but stripping the logic back to core ratings and standard job events proved far more reliable for live sessions. Keeping the rule lean also makes troubleshooting much easier down the road.

Rule Logic Example.

Scenario 1:

IF
(Last Promotion Date is before Lookback Date (36 Months Ago))
AND
(Current Calibration Rating >= 4)
AND
(Previous Calibration Rating >= 4)
AND
(Prior Calibration Rating >= 4)
THEN
Raise Alert using Alert Message. Alert for Lack of Promotion

 Scenario 2:

IF
(Current Calibration Rating <= 2)
AND
(Previous Calibration Rating >= 4)
AND
(Prior Calibration Rating >= 4)
THEN
Raise Alert using “Performance Reduction Alert”

Scenario 3:

IF
(Has Absences In Period = Yes)
AND
(Current Calibration Rating <= 3)
AND
(Previous Calibration Rating >= 4)
AND
(Prior Calibration Rating >= 4)
THEN
Raise Alert using Alert for LOA

 

Rule Assignment

Once the Calibration Alert rule is saved, it must be assigned to the relevant calibration template.
Navigate to Admin Center > Manage Calibration Templates, open the desired template, and locate the Calibration Alert Rules section. Add the newly created rule to the assigned rules list. The rule will now evaluate subjects during any active calibration session using that template.

 

 

Alert Behavior in Live Calibration Sessions

Once the rule is assigned to a calibration template, alerts appear automatically during the session when conditions are met.

Conclusion

Ultimately, SAP SuccessFactors Calibration Alerts provide a powerful mechanism to eliminate systemic blind spots—like the 'Promotion Gap'—and turn subjective reviews into data-driven talent discussions. While implementing this feature requires careful technical alignment, the strategic payoff is immense. By moving away from gut feelings and letting the system surface actionable historical data, organizations can transform their calibration process from a routine administrative task into a truly equitable talent strategy.

entry.title: Re: LOI Concept
entry.content:
Hi Vijay,

LOI - Letter of Intent is mostly used in Govt/PSU and Banking industries. For example, the organization wants to renew the insurance for employees for the new fiscal year. The insurance policy is expired on 31st March. So, the next renewal should be on 1st April. But the organization finalize to renew the insurance policy with the existing insurance company or any new company by 20th of March. As 20th March very near to 31st March and there is only 11 days in hand to prepare all required documents, organization may fail to issue the PO or Contract to the Insurance company before 31st March. To overcome such situation, the Organization will issue a LOI to the Insurance company for proceeding renew of emplyees insurance policy. The Insurance company will go ahead with the LOI and later the PO or Contract or Legal Documents will issue or signed by both partyes.

So, coming to SAP, we can use a new print output of PO/Contract as LOI - Letter of Intent. We can issue the LOI by giving the basic required input in PO/Contract and take print output as LOI. Later we can issue PO/Contract output after complete legal terms and conditions enter.

Thank You,

Ratiprakash Behera

entry.title: RAP: Fiori Elements List Report – How to sort fuzzy search results by relevance score?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am currently working on a requirement using RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming Model) and facing a challenge related to search behavior.

Project Scenario

RAP application based on CDS View Entity

  • UI: Fiori Elements List Report
  • Search enabled using:
    • @search.searchable: true
    • Fuzzy search on material description
  • Backend system: S/4HANA / ABAP Cloud

The fuzzy search works correctly and returns matching records.

Problem

Although the search results are correct, they are not sorted by relevance (matching score).
I want the results to be sorted by how well they match the search text (best match on top).

I tried:

  • @Consumption.ranked: true
    but it does not work for my List Report scenario.

What I found

  • Blogs suggest using AMDP / Custom Entity to calculate search score.
  • However, in RAP:
    • I cannot access the search string entered in the UI
    • The fuzzy score is not exposed in CDS
  • I would prefer to keep a standard CDS-based RAP solution if possible.

My Questions

  1. Is there any standard or recommended way in RAP/CDS to:
    • access fuzzy search relevance score, or
    • sort results by relevance?
  2. Is Custom Entity with AMDP the only supported solution?
  3. Is SAP planning any enhancement in RAP to handle search ranking?
  4. Are there any best-practice alternatives used in real projects?

What I want to avoid

  • Frontend-only sorting
  • Hardcoding logic
  • Unclear or unsupported hacks

Any guidance, confirmation, or reference from SAP experts would be really helpful.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Kind regards,

Mohammad Afrad Husain.

entry.title: RAP : How do you model complex validations & error handling without breaking Fiori UX
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,
I would like to get guidance and best practices from the community and SAP experts.

Project Context

  • Technology: RAP (ABAP Cloud)
  • UI: Fiori Elements (Object Page + List Report)
  • Backend: S/4HANA
  • Business Object with:
    • Draft enabled
    • Multiple child entities
    • Complex business validations

Real Problem Statement

In our project, we have complex validations such as:

  • Cross‑entity checks (header + items)
  • Checks against existing records
  • Conditional validations based on status, user role, or business rules

We implemented validations using:

  • RAP validation methods
  • FAILED, REPORTED, and %msg handling

Technically, this works — but the user experience is not always optimal.

Issues We Are Facing

  • Errors sometimes appear late (only on Save)
  • Messages are valid but not contextual enough for business users
  • Handling multiple error messages becomes confusing
  • Some validations would ideally run earlier, not just on Save
  • We want to avoid:
    • Custom UI logic
    • Non‑standard hacks
    • Breaking RAP principles

Questions to the Community 

How do you design validations in RAP for complex business objects without flooding the user with errors?

  1. What is the recommended balance between:
    • RAP validations
    • Determinations
    • Frontend behavior (without custom UI code)?
  2. Do you follow any patterns for:
    • Grouping related validation messages
    • Showing errors closer to the field instead of generic messages?
  3. Are there real‑project best practices SAP recommends but are not well documented?

What We Want to Achieve

  • Clean RAP implementation
  • Predictable and user‑friendly Fiori behavior
  • Clear error messages that business users understand
  • Scalable design (future rules will be added)

kind regards,

Mohammad Afrad Husain.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices
entry.content:
@MustafaBensan - Please find below response.

1. SAP BDC connect is primarily for data federation scenario (its not ETL), enables bidirectional, zero-copy/delta-share data sharing between SAP BDC and external platforms (Enterprise databricks, Snowflake, GCP etc).

2 and 3  Replication flow is recommended approach to replicate SAP-Managed or Customer-Managed Data Products into target such as GCP.

4. Refer Blog series: SAP Business Data Cloud Series – Part 1: Introduction to Data Products and Part 3.

Happy learning,

Jeet

entry.title: Re: Skipping Invoice Checks for Reversal Events in MRM_CH...
entry.content:
Deart @kienngo,

In MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD, reversal is not provided as a separate ACTION value. SAP provides actions such as Check, Post, Park etc. but not a dedicated Reverse action. Therefore reversal must be excluded based on the reversal fields available in HEADERDATA

The implemented logic now runs the validation only for normal Check and Post actions and skips the validation when HEADERDATA-REVERSEDOCUMENT or HEADERDATA-REVERSEDOCUMENTFISCALYEAR is filled. This allows supplier invoice reversal to proceed, while the check remains active for normal invoice checking and posting. I verified the case in my environment, during the check or post test I received validation

but when I click Reverse the system did not validation

"------------------------------------------------------------

" MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD

" Validate normal Check/Post, but skip reversal

"------------------------------------------------------------

IF ( action = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_action_check

     OR action = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_action_post )

   AND headerdata-reversedocument IS INITIAL

   AND headerdata-reversedocumentfiscalyear IS INITIAL.

  APPEND VALUE #(

    messagetype      = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_messagetype_error

    messageid        = 'MRM_BADI_CLOUD'

    messagenumber    = '001'

    messagevariable1 = 'Posting/checking is not allowed.'

  ) TO messages.

ENDIF.

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP EAM Data Profiling in Utilities: What are your proven technical and business rules?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I'm reaching out to gather insights on understanding what are data profiling best practices specifically within the utilities industry. I'm particularly interested in understanding both technical implementation approaches and business rule perspectives that has worked, I understand this is very specific topic but I'm looking for starting discussion on proven and experience folks who are working or worked in EAM space.

What I'm looking for:

Technical Perspective:

  • What were the first 5–10 proven EAM data quality rules (Completeness, Accuracy, Timeliness & Consistency) you would implement and which would you explicitly defer?
  • What are critical objects in SAP EAM (Equipment, Functional Location, Task list, Maintenance Plan)? 
  • At what point does transactional data profiling become valuable for you?
  • How do you balance strict MDG enforcement (mandatory/blocked fields) vs scored or monitored rules in utilities EAM?
  • Which SAP aligned tools have you found most effective for EAM data profiling and governance and why?

Business Perspective:

  • In SAP S/4HANA Utilities EAM, where have you seen data profiling create the most measurable value? (e.g., maintenance planning reliability, regulatory readiness, asset health or reporting accuracy)
  • How did you determine the right scope for data profiling without over‑engineering it during ECC → S/4HANA transformations? Regulatory compliance considerations affecting data quality rules
  • Which regulatory or compliance drivers (PHMSA, NERC, FERC, safety‑critical assets) directly shaped your data profiling rules—and how do you keep them auditable long‑term?
  • How are you implementing asset‑class‑specific business rules in S/4HANA
    (e.g., valves, mains, transformers) without creating performance or rule‑maintenance issues?

I'd appreciate any experiences, lessons learned, or resources you can share!

Thanks in advance for your insights.

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content:
@oneeyeman1

Your latest comment and attachments point at a smattering of issues ...

  1. The image with the message "The product configuration failed." is a generic message that states something went wrong during the installation; details will have been supplied in other windows/messages and/or in the various installation logs. For the most part this image doesn't tell us anything useful.
  2. The image with the message "The Replication Management Agent failed to start." would seem to indicate that you told the installation process to a) install the HADR product and b) create (at least some of) the HADR components (this would include the Replication Management Agent - RMA); if you really want to know what the issue was with the RMA creation you would need to review the log file(s) mentioned in the message. Having said that ...
  3. In your comment you mention "The error(s) is about SAP HADR.  /  What is this piece?".  From this it sounds like you don't know what HADR is and therefore you probably don't want to install HADR; however, from the bits-n-pieces of details provided it would appear you did ask the installer to not only install the HADR software but to also create (at least some of) the HADR components.
  4. You attempted to manually startup the ASE instance (startserver -f RUN_REINCLOUD) but that failed with the message "kernel basis_dlock: file '/opt/sap/data/master.dat' already in use by an ASE"; as the message states, the ASE instance was already up and running; at that point had you run a 'ps -aef' you should have seen  a process running the /opt/sap/ASE-16_1/bin/dataserver binary; by itself this error message is not an issue ... it's merely telling you the ASE instance is already up and running

-----------------------------

We're not told the series of steps, options and arguments you provided to the installer so I'm going to assume you did something like the following:

1) When asked to choose from the following ASE installation steps:

  • Typical (this is referring to a typical ASE installation and consists of the basics you'll need to get an ASE instance up and running)
  • Typical SAP  ASE, database always-on option (as stated in small print: this is ASE *plus* the High Availability / Disaster Recovery - HADR option; fwiw, 'always-on' == 'HADR'; this is an advanced option that should only be chosen if you are looking to setup a HADR environment)
  • Full (this is referring to all ASE components; does not include always-on==HADR)
  • Custom (same as Full but you get to pick-n-choose which components to install)

From what you've posted it appears you chose to install the Typical SAP  ASE, database always-on option.  (Since you don't know what HADR is, and assuming you just want the barebones basic ASE software installed, you should have chosen the Typical option.)

2) After the software (ASE and HADR in this case) is installed the installer will ask if you want the installer to create some instances of various components (eg, ASE dataserver, backupserver , etc).  I'm guessing you requested to create a basic HADR environment; during the creation of the HADR components you received the error message (attached image) stating there was a problem starting the RMA).  (Again, choosing the Typical option should not install the HADR components, which in turn means the RMA would not be installed/configured, which in turn would eliminate the RMA error message).

3) I'm guessing you also asked the installer to create an ASE instance (named REINCLOUD); since you don't mention any other installer-related error messages I'm going to assume this ASE instance was in fact a) created and b) successfully started since ...

4) After the installer finished (albeit with errors) you chose to manually start the ASE instance (startserver -f RUN_REINCLOUD), but because the installer has already started this ASE instance you were provided with the errorlog message "'/opt/sap/data/master.dat' already in use by an ASE".

-----------------------------

Suggested next steps ...

  1. Make sure all sybase processes are shutdown and/or killed (this includes dataserver and backupserver binaries as well as any HADR-related binaries; if in doubt run 'ps -aef' and look for anything running under the /opt/sap directory, then kill any such processes
  2. Delete the entire directory /opt/sap; you want to start over from scratch with no lingering issues from the previous/failed installation(s).
  3. Run the installer and when it asks you to Choose Install Set, chose the first option - Typical; again, I'm assuming you only want to install the base minimum software to get an ASE instance up and running
  4. After the installer exits, and assuming no errors/issues, and assuming you requested a sample ASE instance to be created, the ASE instance should already be up and running; if the ASE instance is not running for some reason then go ahead and manually start the ASE instance (startserver -f RUN_...)

entry.title: SAP Joule for Consultants at SAP SAPPHIRE - Orlando 2026
entry.content:
At SAP, we build on a strong track record of innovation. With SAP Joule for Consultants, we extend the power of generative AI across our consultant ecosystem, empowering professionals to work more productively.

While our initial focus has been on leveraging the SAP Knowledge Base to support your needs, we also recognize the importance of integrating custom-specific knowledge. In this context, we’re excited to share our latest updates and announcements.

At SAPPHIRE – Orlando 2026, you can meet our experts at sessions

  • FOU2662 - Accelerating Migration and Transformation with SAP Joule for Consultants (5/11 4-4:20pm EDT Theater 18)
  • FOU2663 - How SAP Joule for Consultants Helps Partners Deliver Faster (5/13 2:30-2:50pm EDT Theater 12)

Demo Pods 158 to learn the following:

  • Prompting best practices
  • Accelerating migration and transformation
  • New features and innovation
  • Product vision and evolution
  • Activation process for SAP Joule for Consultants

If you are just about to get started, you can find the quick activation resources below:

More official resources:

  • Help Page – SAP Joule for Consultants
  • SAP Discovery Center Mission – Enable SAP Joule for Consultants
  • SAP Community Blog Post - Getting Started with Joule for Consultants - Setup Guide
  • What’s New – Latest released features (filter with Product – SAP Joule for Consultants)

entry.title: Missing Association After Data Product Deployment in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

A Sales table is modeled as a fact view with an association to a Product dimension (modeled as a separate graphical view). Both views are exposed for consumption. A data product is created on top of these two artifacts - Sales and Product and deployed. And the data product is installed in the target space while both views are available in the target space, the association is missing. Is this by design.

Could you help clarify why this happens. Thanks!
--
KRPK
